question: The theory of 'comparative advantage' (Ricardo) argues that countries should specialize in producing:
options: Goods where they have absolute advantage (can produce more efficiently in absolute terms)

Goods in which they have the lowest opportunity cost — even if one country is absolutely better at producing everything, both countries benefit from specialization and trade; example: if the US produces both wheat and software better than Brazil (absolute advantage in both), both countries still benefit from trade if the US has a relatively larger advantage in software (lower opportunity cost of producing software vs. wheat) — the US specializes in software, Brazil specializes in wheat, they trade, and both consume more of both goods than if each tried to produce everything domestically
Products they have been producing historically for the longest time
Items that their largest trading partner most wants to buy
explanation: Comparative advantage and opportunity cost: the key insight is that specialization and trade creates wealth even when one party is absolutely more efficient at everything. The opportunity cost logic: if a surgeon can also type faster than their assistant, they should still hire the assistant — the surgeon's time is more valuable in the operating room (where no one else can do what they do) than typing. The country-level parallel: even if the US can produce everything more efficiently than Vietnam, Vietnam specializes in labor-intensive manufacturing (where its relative advantage is greatest — lower wages) while the US specializes in capital-intensive and knowledge-intensive production (where its relative advantage is greatest). This specialization produces more total output than autarky (each country producing everything itself). Limitations: comparative advantage assumes full employment (displaced workers find new jobs), ignores distribution of trade gains (some industries lose even as the overall economy gains), and dynamic considerations (infant industry protection).

question: 'Tariffs,' 'quotas,' and 'non-tariff barriers' are all forms of trade protection. How does each work?
options: All three are identical mechanisms that restrict trade in the same way

Tariff: a tax on imported goods — makes imports more expensive, protects domestic producers, generates government revenue, but raises prices for domestic consumers (consumers pay the tariff through higher retail prices); Quota: a physical quantity limit on imports — limits competition from imports but doesn't generate government revenue; often results in higher profits for the limited number of importers allowed to bring goods in (quota rents); Non-tariff barriers: regulatory requirements that discriminate against imports without explicit tariffs — health and safety standards designed to block imports (Japanese auto regulations historically), required testing and certification processes, labeling requirements, government procurement preferences ('Buy American'), and subsidy programs; the WTO (World Trade Organization) has reduced tariffs significantly but non-tariff barriers remain significant trade friction
Only tariffs are legal under international trade law
Tariffs are the most effective trade protection mechanism for all industries
explanation: Trade protection economics: tariffs are a tax that: makes domestic production relatively cheaper, raises revenue for government, but raises prices for all consumers of the protected good. The steel tariff example: US steel tariffs protect ~140,000 US steelworkers but raise steel prices for US manufacturers of cars, appliances, and construction — costing jobs in steel-using industries; academic estimates suggest each job saved in steel costs $500,000–800,000 in higher consumer prices. Quota example: the US sugar quota limits sugar imports to protect US sugar producers, resulting in US sugar prices 2–3× world market prices. The non-tariff barrier example: Japan's regulations requiring inspection of all imported foreign cars (historically) effectively blocked car imports that complied with international standards — the regulatory compliance cost eliminated the price advantage of imports.

question: The 'gravity model' of trade predicts that trade flows between two countries are proportional to their economic size and inversely proportional to the distance between them. What does this imply for international trade strategy?
options: Distance between countries has no effect on bilateral trade volumes

Geographic proximity and economic size are the strongest determinants of bilateral trade: the gravity model's prediction: large, neighboring economies trade more with each other than large, distant economies — Canada and Mexico are the US's largest trading partners (despite much smaller GDPs than China) partly because of geographic proximity; NAFTA/USMCA trade: US trade with Canada and Mexico accounts for 30%+ of total US trade; the strategic implications: (1) Supply chain location: companies locating facilities should consider transportation costs, which correlate with distance; (2) Market entry priority: firms entering international markets should prioritize countries with: large economies (large addressable market) AND geographic/cultural proximity (lower transaction costs); (3) Regional trade blocs (EU, USMCA, ASEAN) are economically rational precisely because they facilitate trade among geographically proximate economies
Trade primarily flows from developed to developing economies regardless of geography
Countries only trade with each other if they have no overlapping product categories
explanation: Gravity model implications for business: supply chain geography has historically followed gravity model predictions — US manufacturers source from Canada and Mexico (proximity), while European manufacturers source from Eastern Europe and North Africa (proximity). The reshoring/nearshoring trend (2020s): after COVID-19 supply chain disruptions, companies are explicitly moving supply chains to physically closer locations — semiconductor manufacturing to Arizona and Ohio (instead of Taiwan), pharmaceutical manufacturing to US/Europe (instead of India). The gravity model's 'distance' concept is broader than physical distance: language distance (English-speaking countries trade more), cultural distance (shared business norms reduce transaction costs), institutional distance (similar legal systems reduce contract enforcement costs). New Zealand trades more with Australia (nearby, similar culture) than its size alone would predict.

question: China's manufacturing competitiveness for electronics assembly is often attributed to 'low labor costs.' A more complete analysis suggests labor costs are only one factor. What actually makes China's manufacturing ecosystem so competitive for Apple's iPhone production?
options: China's manufacturing dominance is explained entirely by labor wage rates

The manufacturing ecosystem advantage exceeds simple labor cost: (1) Supplier density: in Shenzhen/Guangdong, 10,000+ electronics component suppliers are within a day's drive of assembly facilities — a design change can source a new component within 24 hours; in the US, a design change might require 6 weeks of procurement; (2) Skilled manufacturing workforce: China has 500,000+ manufacturing engineers (US has 200,000 for a larger economy) who can staff production ramps; (3) Infrastructure: transportation, power, water, and logistics infrastructure optimized for manufacturing; (4) Scale: TSMC (Taiwan) and Foxconn have factory scales (500,000+ workers at one facility) impossible in the US without decades of investment; (5) Labor cost: significant but declining — wages in Guangdong have risen 5× since 2000; manufacturing has moved to Vietnam and Mexico as China's wages rose; (6) Supply chain integration: Foxconn, TSMC, and suppliers have co-evolved capabilities for 30 years that cannot be replicated in 5 years
Apple should immediately move iPhone manufacturing to the US at no cost impact
Only low-wage countries can manufacture complex electronics products
explanation: Apple's manufacturing geography: Tim Cook's famous quote (2011): 'The US has started to see a decline in the number of tool and die makers. Do you know how many tool and die shops there are in China? ... You can fill multiple football stadiums with tool and die makers.' The Apple manufacturing ecosystem analysis: the Foxconn factory in Zhengzhou (Apple City) employs 300,000+ workers, houses them on-site, and can ramp iPhone production from 0 to 500,000/day in 96 hours. This capability was built through 30+ years of investment. The US CHIPS Act and Apple's component manufacturing: Apple has committed to some US manufacturing (chips from TSMC Arizona) but at costs that are 30–50% higher than Taiwan production. The supply chain relocation reality: moving even 10% of iPhone production out of China takes years and costs billions. The supplier ecosystem (the 200+ iPhone component suppliers located in Asia) can't be relocated in a decade.

question: The US-China trade war (2018–present) involved escalating tariffs (25% on $300B+ in goods) and technology restrictions (export controls on semiconductors, restrictions on Huawei). What economic and strategic logic underlies these measures, and what are the costs?
options: Trade tariffs always improve economic welfare for the country imposing them

Strategic trade policy vs. economic welfare trade-off: the tariff rationale: (1) Rebalance trade deficit (the US-China goods trade deficit was $350B in 2017); (2) Force China to abandon mercantilist practices (government subsidies to domestic industries, forced technology transfer requirements for market access, currency manipulation concerns); (3) Protect strategic industries (steel, aluminum, semiconductors) where dependence on China creates military/economic vulnerability. Economic costs: (1) US consumers pay higher prices for goods with Chinese inputs (tariffs are essentially a consumption tax); (2) US exporters lose sales to retaliating Chinese tariffs (soybeans, Boeing aircraft, US corn); (3) US companies with China supply chains face higher costs or expensive relocation; academic estimates: the 2018 tariffs cost US businesses and consumers $51B/year. Strategic rationale: the semiconductor export controls (preventing TSMC from selling advanced chips to Huawei and Chinese military) reflect non-economic national security logic that traditional trade theory doesn't capture — some industries are worth protecting regardless of economic cost
Trade tariffs never have any negative economic consequences
Trade restrictions between large economies have minimal economic effects
explanation: Trade war economics: the standard economic result (comparative advantage) predicts tariffs reduce overall welfare. The political economy of trade: producers who benefit from tariffs are concentrated (steel workers, steel companies) and politically organized; consumers who pay the tariff cost are diffuse (hundreds of millions of people paying slightly more for goods containing steel). Political economy thus tends toward protectionism: the concentrated benefits (steel industry political campaign contributions) outweigh the diffuse costs (individual consumer impact is small). The strategic trade dimension: the semiconductor export controls reflect a genuine national security judgment — advanced AI chips (NVIDIA A100s) are useful for military applications; the US government decided the commercial loss is worth the strategic gain of slowing Chinese military AI capability. Whether this judgment is correct is an ongoing policy debate. The supply chain rerouting: since the tariffs, US imports from Vietnam, Mexico, and India have increased significantly as companies use third-country production to avoid tariffs — suggesting tariffs reduced US-China trade but increased total US imports from other sources.

question: A British apparel manufacturer exports to the EU under the zero-tariff access provided by EU membership. After Brexit (UK left the EU in 2020), the UK faces a 12% tariff on clothing exports to the EU and additional customs documentation requirements. The manufacturer's production costs increase 18% (combined tariff and compliance costs). What business decisions must the manufacturer now evaluate?
options: The manufacturer should immediately stop all exports to the EU

Market access vs. cost structure reassessment: the post-Brexit math: 18% cost increase on EU exports means: (1) Either absorb the cost (reduce margins); (2) Pass it to EU customers (higher prices → lose EU market share); (3) Move some or all EU production to an EU country (Ireland, Netherlands) to regain zero-tariff access; (4) Refocus on non-EU markets (US, Canada, Australia — no Brexit tariff impact); the factory in EU option: manufacturing in Ireland (EU member, common language, historically strong trade ties with UK) would restore zero-tariff access to EU; cost analysis: Irish manufacturing cost premium vs. UK + 12% tariff — the tariff may make EU location financially attractive despite higher base costs; the competitive assessment: competitors from France, Italy, and Germany (all EU members) don't face the 12% tariff — structural competitive disadvantage for any UK-based EU exporter; strategic reframe: Brexit has effectively created a structural reason to reconsider the UK as a manufacturing base for EU-targeted products
Tariffs always make manufacturing relocation the correct response
Brexit had no practical effect on UK-EU trade
explanation: Brexit trade impact: the Centre for European Reform estimated Brexit reduced UK goods trade by 15% relative to what it would have been in 2021. The apparel industry specifically was severely impacted: UK clothing exports to the EU fell significantly post-Brexit due to tariff friction and non-tariff barriers (rules of origin requirements for EU tariff exemption were particularly complex for apparel). The rules of origin problem: EU free trade agreements typically require that goods be 'substantially manufactured' in the UK to qualify for zero tariffs — an apparel company using Asian fabric and EU accessories may not meet the rules of origin threshold, paying the tariff even on UK-made products. The practical responses: some UK manufacturers relocated operations to EU member states (especially Ireland and Netherlands); others shifted focus to UK domestic market and non-EU markets; others absorbed margin compression. The Brexit impact on manufacturing location demonstrates that tariff structures are a fundamental input to supply chain and factory location decisions.

question: The WTO (World Trade Organization) provides a framework for international trade rules. When the US imposed steel tariffs in 2002 (under Bush) and again in 2018 (under Trump), the EU challenged these at the WTO. What does the WTO dispute resolution process reveal about the governance of international trade?
options: The WTO can force member countries to comply with its rulings through military action

The WTO provides a rules-based forum for resolving trade disputes, but enforcement depends on member compliance: the WTO dispute mechanism: (1) Consultations between parties; (2) Panel decision; (3) Appellate Body review; (4) Authorized retaliatory tariffs if the losing party doesn't comply. The 2002 steel case: the WTO ruled the US tariffs were inconsistent with WTO obligations; the US withdrew the tariffs in 2003 in part because the WTO authorized the EU to impose $2B in retaliatory tariffs on politically sensitive US exports (oranges from Florida, steel from Pennsylvania — targeted to create domestic US political pressure). The 2018 case: the US used 'national security' justification (Section 232); the WTO's Appellate Body was hamstrung — the US blocked new appointments to the Appellate Body, preventing the body from functioning to adjudicate the appeal. The broader governance point: international trade rules work when: major powers choose to comply because the system benefits them; enforcement is through reciprocal retaliation; national security exceptions are broadly written, potentially undermining the rules-based system
The WTO has never resolved a trade dispute between major economies
WTO rulings automatically take effect without requiring member country action
explanation: WTO enforcement mechanism: the key enforcement tool is authorized retaliation — the complaining country gets permission to impose tariffs on the offending country's exports. This works when: the retaliation is economically significant (enough to create domestic political pressure to comply) and when the offending party cares about its WTO reputation. The US blocking of the Appellate Body (2019–present): by refusing to approve new Appellate Body members, the US effectively blocked any review of WTO panel decisions, creating an adjudication gap that other countries have exploited. The 'national security' loophole: Article XXI of GATT allows countries to impose trade restrictions for national security reasons — the WTO has historically been reluctant to second-guess national security claims, creating a broad exception that can be used to justify almost any restriction. The geopolitical implication: as US-China strategic competition intensifies, trade measures increasingly are used as strategic tools rather than purely economic ones, testing the limits of the WTO's rules-based system.
